_meta.purpose: Provenance archive for the real-agent token-cost addendum (REALAGENT-PREREGISTRATION.md). One record per real decision call: the model requested, the structured decision returned (verbatim), the real output-token delta (via the Workflow harness's own before/after token accounting around that single call), and the outcome once fed through the witness (governed) or the plain resource map (baseline). Differs from ../replication-2026-07-17/decision-logs.json in provenance method: those records were hand-extracted from raw agent transcripts (api_message_id, prompt_sha256); these are extracted from the workflow run's own progress/journal metadata. That field set is not fabricated here to match the old shape.

decision.why: No evidence of contention or risk; requesting now is the default rational move absent contrary info.
No information suggests contention or risk; requesting the needed resource now is the direct path to completing the task.
No evidence of contention or risk; requesting the needed resource directly is the rational default absent contrary information.
Need N2 to complete task. No information suggests deadlock or unavailability. Requesting is standard protocol; system handles resource conflicts.

Without knowledge of other actors' resource holdings or current N3 availability, requesting could trigger deadlock (if another actor holds N3 and needs my N2). Waiting preserves safety in this single-decision trial until more information emerges.

Requesting N1 immediately could trigger deadlock if the actor holding N1 also needs N3 (which I possess). With incomplete system information and only one decision, waiting is the safer strategy to allow the system to progress and avoid circular dependencies.
First attempt, no evidence of contention or deadlock risk; trying directly is the natural first move.
No information indicates contention or risk; first attempt should just try to acquire N3 directly.
First attempt, no evidence of contention or conflict; requesting is the default reasonable action.
Immediate re-request likely refused again; waiting avoids contention/livelock while keeping option to retry.
No info on holder or release time; immediate re-request likely fails again, and abandoning is premature.
N1 just refused; retrying immediately risks thrashing. No info suggests urgency or permanent unavailability, so waiting is prudent.
No info N2 will free soon; immediate re-request risks thrashing. Waiting preserves option without giving up permanently.
Just refused; immediate re-request risks contention/livelock. No evidence it's gone for good, so wait rather than abstain.
No new info since refusal; immediate retry wastes cycles, and permanent abstain is unwarranted without evidence.
First attempt to acquire N2. No information suggests unavailability. Needed to complete task. Request immediately.
First attempt, need N3 to complete task, no information suggesting contention or deadlock risk.
First attempt to acquire N1. I need it to finish my task. No conflicts or constraints indicated. Requesting now is the prudent initial strategy.
Immediate re-request will likely fail again. Waiting gives the resource holder time to release N2, maintaining opportunity to acquire it and complete the task.
Request now will fail again—N3 still held. Waiting costs nothing and allows holder to release it. Abstaining abandons task completion.
Immediate retry is futile since N1 was just refused. Waiting gives the holder time to release it, preserving my task completion option without wasting a request.
Previous request refused; immediate retry unlikely to succeed. Waiting allows N2 holder to finish and release it.

Previous request for N3 was refused with no new information about the holder or release timeline. Requesting again immediately is unlikely to succeed. Waiting allows time for the resource to potentially be released by its current holder, preserving the possibility of eventual task completion without prematurely surrendering (abstain) or futilely repeating a failed action.
Previous refusal just occurred. Immediate retry would likely fail identically. Waiting preserves retry option without wasting attempts. No urgency stated to justify permanent abstention.
